params.text:
Clean showerheads and taps

Because scale and biofilm feed and shelter legionella, keeping fittings clean is another important control. Showerheads and spray taps are a particular focus, as they combine standing water, warmth and the very spray that carries droplets into the air.

  • Descaling: removing the scale that builds up in showerheads and taps.
  • Cleaning: washing away biofilm and debris.
  • Routine: doing this on the schedule your service sets.

How cleaning helps

Over time, limescale and a slimy biofilm build up inside showerheads and around taps, giving legionella both food and a protected place to grow. Cleaning and descaling these fittings on a regular schedule removes that build-up and keeps the spray cleaner and safer.

If you are asked to clean or descale a showerhead, follow your service's method and schedule, and record that you have done it. As with flushing, this is a straightforward task, but doing it properly and on time makes a genuine difference. If a showerhead is heavily furred with scale, or a fitting looks dirty or slimy, report it so it can be dealt with.

params.question: Why does your service ask you to descale and clean showerheads regularly?

answers.text: Scale and biofilm feed and shelter legionella, so removing them keeps the spray cleaner and safer.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Yes. Cleaning away scale and biofilm removes the food and shelter that let legionella grow.

answers.text: Clean showerheads make the water come out hotter.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Cleaning is about removing scale and biofilm, not about water temperature.

answers.text: It is only about appearance and has no effect on safety.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: It is a genuine safety control, not just about how things look.

answers.text: It removes the need to keep hot water hot or cold water cold.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Cleaning works alongside temperature control, not instead of it.
